1: A revelation of God: "All this I have told you so that you will not fall away." The purpose of the farewell discourses — the foot-washing, the vine, the love command, the promise of the Spirit — is pastoral: the disciples should not stumble when persecution comes. The foreknowledge the Son has shared is not meant to produce anxiety but stability. Those who know in advance what is coming, why it is happening, and who will sustain them through it are equipped to endure what those without this knowledge would abandon under pressure.
2: A revelation of God: "the time is coming when anyone who kills you will think they are offering a service to God." The most chilling form of religious persecution: violence committed in the name of divine service. This anticipates both the Jewish persecution of early Christians (Acts 7:58 — the stoning of Stephen) and the Roman persecutions framed as civic-religious duty. Jesus warns of this specifically so that when the disciples face religiously motivated killing, they understand that the persecutors are acting from the same darkness that crucified the Son — mistaking the will of the world-ruler for the will of God.
3: A revelation of God: "They will do such things because they have not known the Father or me." The ignorance of the persecutors — they do not know the Father or the Son — is their only partial excuse and their comprehensive condemnation simultaneously. They act in the name of God while remaining ignorant of who God is; they persecute the disciples of the Son while denying the Son. The one who truly knows the Father would recognize the Son; the one who truly knows the Son would recognize those who bear his name. The root of religious violence is theological ignorance of this depth.
4: A revelation of God: "so that when their time comes you will remember that I warned you about them." The pastoral function of prophecy — equipping the disciples to interpret their suffering when it arrives — is grounded in the foreknowledge of Christ who sees what is coming and prepares his followers for it. The remembered prophecy is not a consolation but a reorientation: when the disciples suffer, they should not conclude that God has abandoned them but that Christ's prediction has been confirmed, which grounds continued trust.
5: A revelation of God: "None of you asks me, Where are you going?" The disciples are absorbed in their grief at the prospect of Christ's departure (v6) and not asking the question that would reframe their experience. Where he is going — to the Father — is the key to understanding why his departure is not loss but gain. The Evangelist uses the disciples' silence to open the teaching of vv7–11: the direction of departure matters more than the fact of it. Going to the Father enables the sending of the Spirit, which enables everything that follows.
6: A theme: "you are filled with grief because I have said these things." The disciples' grief is genuine and human. The Evangelist does not dismiss it; Jesus addresses it directly in what follows. The grief that fills the disciples is the grief of those who have understood that departure is coming but not yet understood what the departure accomplishes. The same grief-to-joy transition that Jesus describes for childbirth (v21) will be their experience: the grief of the cross will become the joy of the resurrection morning.
7: A direct revelation: "it is for your good that I am going away. Unless I go away, the Advocate will not come to you; but if I go, I will send him to you." The departure of the incarnate Christ is the precondition for the Spirit's coming, and the Spirit's presence is a greater good than the physical presence of the pre-crucifixion Jesus. The earthly Jesus was geographically limited; the Spirit-mediated Christ is universally present. The glorified Christ sending the Spirit enables the mission that the unglorious Christ cannot alone accomplish. This is the logic of Pentecost: the ascension of the Son enables the descent of the Spirit.
8: A direct revelation: "When he comes, he will prove the world to be in the wrong about sin and righteousness and judgment." The Spirit's ministry in the world is a forensic one — establishing the truth about three realities that the world has misunderstood. The three convictions the Spirit works correspond to the three Christological claims that the world has denied: the world is wrong about what sin is (v9), what righteousness is (v10), and who has been judged (v11).
9: A direct revelation: "about sin, because people do not believe in me." Sin in the Johannine framework is not primarily moral failure but the rejection of the Son. The ultimate sin — the one the Spirit must convict the world about — is unbelief in Christ. All other sins are symptoms of this root: the refusal to receive the one God has sent. The Spirit convicts the world that its fundamental posture toward God is wrong because it is a posture of rejection toward the Son whom God gave for its life (John 3:16).
10: A direct revelation: "about righteousness, because I am going to the Father, where you can see me no longer." The Son's going to the Father is the Father's vindication of his righteousness — the resurrection and ascension are the Father's declaration that the Son was righteous and his accusers were wrong. The Spirit convicts the world about righteousness by pointing to the ascended Christ: the one they condemned as a blasphemer has been received by God. The righteousness the Spirit reveals is not moral perfection in the abstract but the vindicated person of Christ, declared righteous by the Father's raising him.
11: A direct revelation: "about judgment, because the prince of this world now stands condemned." The cross — which looked like Christ's condemnation — is in fact the condemnation of the prince of this world. The Spirit convicts the world that the judgment it feared (its own condemnation) has been executed on the adversary, and the one it executed (Christ) has been vindicated. The cross is the moment of the world-ruler's defeat (John 12:31), and the Spirit reveals this reversal to the world: the one who seemed to lose is the victor; the one who seemed to win stands condemned.
12: A revelation of God: "I have much more to say to you, more than you can now bear." The restraint of the Son in the pre-resurrection period is itself a form of pastoral care: the disciples cannot yet receive what the Spirit will bring after the glorification. The limitation is not the Son's but the disciples': they are not yet constituted to receive the full revelation. The coming of the Spirit — and the resurrection that precedes it — will be the transformation that makes them able to receive what the earthly discourses can only begin to communicate.
13: A direct revelation: "when he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will guide you into all the truth." The Spirit's guiding into truth is the Christological continuation of what Jesus has been doing in the farewell discourses — making the Father known through the Son. The Spirit does not add to the content of the Son's revelation but leads the disciples into the full depth of what the Son has disclosed. The truth the Spirit reveals is not new truth but the same truth — the whole truth of Christ — that the disciples were not yet able to bear (v12). The canon of apostolic Scripture is the Spirit's fulfillment of this promise.
14: A direct revelation: "He will glorify me because it is from me that he will receive what he will make known to you." The Spirit's work is inherently Christological: he glorifies the Son by taking what belongs to the Son and making it known. The Spirit does not point to himself; he points to Christ. Every true work of the Spirit in the world — conviction of sin, regeneration, illumination, sanctification — is a work that points to and glorifies Christ. The Spirit is the Spirit of Christ (Romans 8:9; 1 Peter 1:11), and his ministry is the extension of Christ's own glorification.
15: A direct revelation: "All that belongs to the Father is mine. That is why I said the Spirit will receive from me what he will make known to you." The Son's identity with the Father — "all that belongs to the Father is mine" — is stated with maximum comprehensiveness. The shared possession of the Father and Son includes the truth the Spirit mediates: what the Father has, the Son has; what the Son has, the Spirit discloses. The chain of revelation (Father → Son → Spirit → disciples) is grounded in the Trinitarian sharing of all things between Father and Son.
16: A revelation of God: "In a little while you will see me no more, and then after a little while you will see me." The "little while" before and after refers to the death (a little while to the crucifixion) and the resurrection (a little while back to appearing). The disciples will experience the loss of seeing Jesus (the crucifixion and burial) and the restoration of sight (the resurrection appearances). The time frame is exact: three days. The disciples cannot yet grasp this, which is why their confusion in vv17–18 is genuine.
17: A theme: the disciples' mutual puzzlement — "what does he mean by 'a little while'?" — shows that the resurrection framework has not yet been given to them. They are trying to interpret the statement within what they know, and what they know does not include the resurrection. The Evangelist preserves their incomprehension as historically accurate and theologically significant: the disciples who will become the apostles are right now unable to understand what their own preaching will later hinge on.
18: A theme: "We don't understand what he is saying." The honest admission of incomprehension is the posture the Evangelist has consistently valued over false confidence. The disciples do not pretend to understand; they confess they do not. This is the same posture that opened the space for revelation in John 13:25 (the Beloved Disciple asking plainly about the betrayer) and John 14:5 (Thomas asking plainly about the way). Jesus' response (v19) will address the question rather than rebuking it.
19: A revelation of God: Jesus perceives their desire to ask — divine insight into their hearts (John 2:25; John 6:61). He addresses the question before they articulate it, which is itself a sign of who he is. The response in vv20–22 will not explain the "little while" analytically but will reframe it with the birth-pain image: the grief and the joy are the same event experienced in two moments. The Son's pastoral response to their confusion is not a lecture but an image that holds the grief and the joy together.
20: A revelation of God: "You will weep and mourn while the world rejoices. You will grieve, but your grief will turn to joy." The cross will be the moment of the disciples' maximum grief and the world's apparent victory. The resurrection will reverse both: the disciples' grief becomes joy; the world's rejoicing will discover it was premature. The reversal is the pattern of the entire biblical narrative: the Servant's suffering becomes the many's justification (Isaiah 53:11); the cross becomes resurrection; death becomes life. Isaiah 35:10 anticipated this: "sorrow and sighing will flee away."
21: A revelation of God: "A woman giving birth to a child has pain because her time has come; but when her baby is born she forgets the anguish because of her joy." The birth-pain image for the transition from suffering to glory is the OT's consistent metaphor for the messianic age (Isaiah 26:17–19 — resurrection from the dead through birth-pain; Isaiah 66:7–9 — Zion's birth-pain producing a nation). The disciples' grief at the cross is the birth-pain that precedes the joy of the resurrection. The anguish is real; it is also temporary and productive — it is the birth of the new creation.
22: A direct revelation: "I will see you again and you will rejoice, and no one will take away your joy." The resurrection appearances are the fulfillment of this promise — Jesus will see them again, and their joy will be the indestructible joy of those who have seen the risen Lord. "No one will take away your joy" is the promise of the permanence of resurrection-joy — not dependent on circumstances, not revocable by the world, not subject to the returning grief of loss. The joy of the resurrection is the eschatological joy that remains.
23: A revelation of God: "In that day you will no longer ask me anything. Truly, truly, I tell you, my Father will give you whatever you ask in my name." The post-resurrection era is the era of direct access — the disciples will not need to ask Jesus questions (their understanding will be complete through the Spirit) but will ask the Father directly in Jesus' name. The mediation of prayer through Christ's name is not an obstacle but the ground of the access: the disciples' relationship to the Father is established through the Son, and prayer in his name means praying as those who belong to the Son.
24: A revelation of God: "Until now you have not asked for anything in my name." The era of praying "in my name" has not yet opened — it will open at the resurrection and the giving of the Spirit. The disciples are at the threshold of a new mode of prayer that requires the crucified, risen, and ascended Christ to be its ground. The completeness of joy that prayer in Christ's name produces is the fullness of the covenant relationship now available through the Son's finished work.
25: A revelation of God: "a time is coming when I will no longer use this kind of language but will tell you plainly about my Father." The figurative speech of the vine, the birth-pain, the "little while" will give way to plain disclosure after the resurrection — both through the post-resurrection teaching (Acts 1:3 — "he appeared to them over a period of forty days and spoke about the kingdom of God") and through the Spirit's illumination of what the figurative language was pointing to. The resurrection is the hermeneutical key that converts parabolic speech into plain revelation.
26: A revelation of God: "In that day you will ask in my name. I am not saying that I will ask the Father on your behalf." The statement clarifies the nature of Christ's intercession: he does not have to overcome the Father's reluctance or compensate for the Father's distance from the disciples. The Father himself loves those who belong to the Son. The Son's intercession is not needed to make the Father willing; the Father is already willing because the disciples are already his. The intercession is the formal channel of the relational access that the Father himself desires to give.
27: A revelation of God: "the Father himself loves you because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God." The Father's direct love for the disciples is grounded in their love for and belief in the Son. The relational chain is complete: those who are united to the Son by love and faith are united to the Father who loves the Son. The disciples' faith is not merely a technique for accessing divine power; it is the relational bond that places them within the sphere of the Father's own covenantal love.
28: A direct revelation: "I came from the Father and entered the world; now I am leaving the world and going back to the Father." The fullest single-sentence statement of Christological ontology in the Gospel: the complete arc of the incarnation — origin in the Father, descent into the world, and return to the Father. The four movements (from, entered, leaving, going back) correspond to the preexistence, incarnation, death/ascension, and glorification at the Father's right hand. The disciples have been hearing this in figurative language; here it is plain. The Son who comes from the Father is the same Son who returns to the Father — no loss of identity in the descent, no diminishment in the return.
29: A theme: "Now you are speaking clearly and without figures of speech." The disciples feel they have finally understood — the plain statement of v28 seems to remove all ambiguity. Their confidence is touching and, as the next verses will show, premature. They understand the statement; they have not yet understood the person. The comprehension of Christological propositions is not the same as the faith that endures through the cross. Their confidence will be tested within hours.
30: A revelation of God: "Now we can see that you know all things and that you do not even need to have anyone ask you questions. This makes us believe that you came from God." The disciples' statement of belief — "we believe that you came from God" — is a correct Christological confession. The ground they give (his foreknowledge) is also correct. But Jesus' response (v31) does not ratify their confidence; he questions its durability. The belief that cannot survive the cross is belief in a Christ who has not yet been crucified.
31: A revelation of God: "Do you now believe?" The question is gentle but searching. The Evangelist presents Jesus as testing the quality of the disciples' faith — not whether they have correct beliefs but whether those beliefs are rooted deeply enough to endure what is coming. The question anticipates the scattering of v32: the faith they are claiming will be tested within the hour. The question is also for the reader: the faith evoked by the farewell discourses must be tested by the passion narrative that follows to see whether it is the faith John 20:31 calls for.
32: A revelation of God: "you will be scattered, each to your own home. You will leave me all alone. Yet I am not alone, for my Father is with me." The scattering of the disciples is both predicted and absorbed into the Son's certainty of the Father's presence. Zechariah 13:7 ("strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered") will be fulfilled (cited in Matthew 26:31; Mark 14:27). But the Son is not left desolate even in the abandonment: "I am not alone, for my Father is with me." The one who is about to be abandoned by his disciples and cry out from the cross (Matthew 27:46) nevertheless grounds his identity in the Father's unbroken presence. Johannine theology does not diminish the reality of the cross; it frames it within the Son's eternal relationship to the Father.
33: A direct revelation: "I have overcome the world." The verb is perfect tense (nenikēka) — a completed action with ongoing results. The victory is accomplished before the cross, not after it, because the cross is not the world's victory over the Son but the Son's defeat of the world's power over humanity. The entire system of sin, death, judgment, and the adversary's dominion is overcome in the person of the Son who is about to walk through it all without being held by it. The peace he promises (v27) is grounded in this accomplished victory. The disciples will "have trouble" in the world (the word for trouble is the same as the disciples' grief at the cross), but they can "take heart" because the one who has overcome the world is within them.
